title: Why the Heart of India’s Indigenous Fighter Ecosystem Will be American for Several Decades to Come
gist: India's push for an indigenous fighter-jet ecosystem (AMCA, LCA Mk2, AMCA-Mk1) is structurally dependent on the American General Electric F414 engine, and stalled GE-HAL negotiations over a tripled per-engine cost are delaying an already-late program.

themes.theme: Engine dependency in India's fighter program

insights.claim: India is negotiating with U.S. manufacturer General Electric to buy F414 jet engines for the five prototypes of its fifth-generation Advanced Medium Combat Aircraft (AMCA), but the talks have stalled because the per-engine cost has tripled.
insights.speaker: Dinakar Peri
insights.so_what: A tripled unit cost on a critical program input signals both GE's pricing leverage and a concrete near-term delay risk for AMCA's prototype timeline.
insights.locator: India is in talks with U.S. engine manufacturer General Electric

insights.claim: The same General Electric F414 engine has been chosen to power not only the AMCA but also the Mk2 variant of the indigenous single-engine Light Combat Aircraft (LCA) and the twin-engine AMCA-Mk1.
insights.speaker: Dinakar Peri
insights.so_what: A single U.S. engine underpins three separate 'indigenous' airframe programs, meaning any GE pricing or supply disruption cascades across India's entire next-generation fighter fleet.
insights.locator: The F414 engine has also been chosen to power

insights.claim: The larger commercial deal under negotiation between GE and Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) for LCA Mk2 production is separate from, and unaffected by, the cost escalation specifically tied to the 15 off-the-shelf engines the Aeronautical Development Agency (ADA) is buying to power the AMCA prototypes.
insights.speaker: Dinakar Peri
insights.so_what: Distinguishes two separate GE-India negotiating tracks investors should watch separately: the AMCA prototype engine buy (currently stalled) versus the larger HAL Mk2 production deal (still in commercial stage).
insights.locator: officials in the know clarified that the larger deal

insights.claim: Because AMCA, LCA Mk2, and AMCA-Mk1 all rely on the same American F414 engine, the core of India's 'indigenous' fighter ecosystem will remain American-sourced propulsion for the foreseeable future, regardless of the self-reliance framing around the airframes themselves.
insights.speaker: Dinakar Peri
insights.so_what: Structural U.S. leverage over India's fighter-modernization timeline persists even as India markets these programs as indigenous — relevant to defense-supply-chain and India-U.S. relations positioning.
insights.locator: The jet engine remains the biggest stumbling block
